topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper seemed to bleed into the white page, a stark, undeniable stain. Liam stared at the 'D-'. It felt heavier than any textbook he’d ever carried. His hands, usually steady when tackling complex equations, began to tremble slightly as he traced the letter with a fingertip. He shoved the paper into his backpack with unnecessary force, the zipper groaning in protest. The fluorescent lights of the library seemed too bright, magnifying the dull ache behind his eyes. He walked toward the exit, the sound of his own footsteps echoing unnervingly loud in the cavernous space. Every corner seemed to hold another judgment, another failure waiting to be cataloged. He just wanted to be anywhere else, anywhere but here, under the weight of that single, damning mark.

[story 2]

"Another one," Sarah muttered, the words tasting like ash in her mouth. She slumped onto the worn vinyl of the cafeteria bench, letting her head fall back against the cool, hard surface. The numerical score mocked her from the screen of her laptop: 58%. It wasn't just a low mark; it was a complete erasure of the effort she’d poured into the project. A tight knot formed in her stomach, constricting her chest until breathing felt shallow. She picked at a loose thread on her sleeve, her gaze fixed on the pattern, unable to focus on anything else. The ambient chatter of the room dissolved into a dull roar. She just wanted to disappear into the noise, to become invisible until the next assignment arrived.

[story 3]

Mark reread the feedback, the words swimming before his eyes. The professor’s critique was terse, clinical, and utterly damning. He slowly lowered the printed sheet, his shoulders slumping forward as if gravity had suddenly increased tenfold. He ran a hand through his already messy hair, the gesture jerky and uncontrolled. A deep, heavy sigh escaped him, a sound that seemed too large for the quiet room. He looked around the lecture hall, at the other students diligently taking notes, and felt a profound disconnect. His own internal engine seemed to have stalled. He picked up his pen, but the tip hovered over the paper, refusing to commit to another word. It felt like trying to lift a boulder.
